children.name: Bufo
children.description: The archetypal toads of Eurasia — warty, terrestrial, slow-moving, and largely nocturnal. The Common Toad is Sweden's only toad, gathering in vast numbers at traditional breeding ponds each spring, often returning to the exact pond where it was born. Toxic parotoid glands behind the eyes deter most predators. Unlike frogs, toads walk rather than hop.

children.name: Bufo bufo
children.description: The quintessential European toad; migrates hundreds of metres to natal pond in early spring; toxic parotoid glands produce bufadienolide poisons that deter most predators; male smaller than female and clasps her for days during amplexus.

children.name: Bufo spinosus
children.description: Recently separated from B. bufo; distinguished by more prominent dorsal tubercles giving a noticeably spiny texture; found across southwestern Europe including the Iberian Peninsula, France, and northwestern Africa.

children.name: Bufo gargarizans
children.description: Commonest toad across East Asia, ranging from Russia's Far East to Southeast Asia; skin secretions (chan su) have been used in traditional Chinese medicine for millennia as a cardiotonic drug.

children.name: Bufo verrucosissimus
children.description: A large warty toad of the Caucasus and northeastern Anatolia, inhabiting humid montane forests near streams; closely related to B. bufo but larger and more heavily tuberculate.

children.name: Bufo japonicus
children.description: Widespread across Japan's main islands; a large terrestrial toad that breeds explosively in late-winter ponds and rice paddies, producing long strings of eggs.

children.name: Bufo bankorensis
children.description: Endemic to Taiwan from lowlands to high mountains; recognised by reddish warts on the dorsum and a pale vertebral stripe; the island's most commonly encountered toad.

children.name: Bufo torrenticola
children.description: A Japanese toad adapted to fast mountain streams, where its tadpoles develop sucker-like mouths to cling to rocks; restricted to clear, cold rivers of Honshu.

children.name: Bufo tibetanus
children.description: A high-altitude specialist found across the Tibetan Plateau and adjacent Qinghai-Sichuan region, often above 4000 m elevation. Its low metabolic rate and tolerance for near-freezing temperatures make it one of the world's highest-living toads.

children.name: Bufotes
children.description: The green toads of the Palearctic — often strikingly patterned in emerald-green marbling on a pale ground. Most are found across Central Asia, the Middle East, and southern Europe, with several species adapted to highly arid habitats. The genus was split from Bufo based on molecular data.

children.name: Bufotes viridis
children.description: Handsomely marbled in green on cream; extremely drought-tolerant and able to breed in brackish water; one of Europe's most adaptable anurans, thriving in gardens, parks, and steppes across a vast Palearctic range.

children.name: Bufotes boulengeri
children.description: Distributed across North Africa and the Iberian Peninsula; named in honour of herpetologist George Albert Boulenger; tolerates highly arid conditions and breeds in temporary pools after winter rains.

children.name: Bufotes pewzowi
children.description: A Central Asian green toad ranging across Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, and western China; named after the Russian explorer Nikolai Pewzow; adapts readily to irrigated agricultural landscapes.

children.name: Bufotes sitibundus
children.description: Widespread across Turkey, the Levant, and the Caucasus foothills; formerly treated as a subspecies of B. viridis; occupies a broad range of habitats from coastal lowlands to montane meadows.

children.name: Anaxyrus
children.description: The North American radiation of true toads — roughly 22 species endemic to the continent, filling habitats from desert dunes to boreal forests.

children.name: Anaxyrus americanus
children.description: Familiar garden visitor across eastern North America; males produce a prolonged melodic trill lasting up to 30 seconds; consumes enormous numbers of pest insects.

children.name: Anaxyrus boreas
children.description: Once abundant across western mountain lakes; now declining sharply due to chytrid fungus Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis; unlike most toads, lacks a vocal sac and does not call loudly.

children.name: Anaxyrus fowleri
children.description: Eastern North American toad of sandy habitats including beaches and floodplains; short nasal 'waaah' call; named after Massachusetts naturalist Samuel Page Fowler.

children.name: Anaxyrus woodhousii
children.description: Wide-ranging western North American toad with a pale mid-dorsal stripe; hybridises with A. americanus in contact zones; named for naturalist and physician Samuel Washington Woodhouse.

children.name: Anaxyrus terrestris
children.description: Toad of the southeastern US with distinctive prominent cranial crests ending in knobs; common across the sandy coastal plain from Virginia to Louisiana.

children.name: Anaxyrus houstonensis
children.description: Critically endangered toad endemic to a few counties of south-central Texas, dependent on deep sandy soils for burrowing; among the first US amphibians protected under the Endangered Species Act in 1970.

children.name: Anaxyrus quercicus
children.description: The smallest North American toad at under 33 mm; pine-oak flatwoods of the southeastern US; call resembles a high-pitched peeping chick and breeding choruses are deafening.

children.name: Anaxyrus punctatus
children.description: A small flat-bodied toad of rocky desert canyons in the southwestern US and Mexico; flattened body allows it to wedge into rock crevices; breeds in temporary rain pools with a high-pitched sustained trill.

children.name: Anaxyrus cognatus
children.description: Large toad of open grasslands and agricultural fields from the Canadian prairies to Mexico; explosive breeder triggered by heavy rains; males produce a metallic clatter lasting up to an hour.

children.name: Anaxyrus speciosus
children.description: Endemic to the short-grass prairies and mesquite shrublands of Texas, New Mexico, and adjacent Mexico; fossorial outside of the rainy breeding season; call is a series of short, metallic buzzes.

children.name: Anaxyrus debilis
children.description: A small, flattened toad of arid grasslands and desert scrub in the south-central US and Mexico; attractively patterned with green spots or reticulations on a pale ground; breeds in pools and streams after monsoon rains.

children.name: Anaxyrus exsul
children.description: One of North America's rarest toads, entirely restricted to a few alkaline springs in Deep Springs Valley, California; striking dark coloration with white-edged warts; federally listed as Endangered with a population in the low thousands.

children.name: Rhinella
children.description: The largest Neotropical toad genus (~90 species), occupying habitats from Amazonian rainforest to Andean high paramo. Many bear enlarged parotoid glands and cranial bony ridges.

children.name: Rhinella marina
children.description: Catastrophically invasive in Australia, Hawaii, and the Caribbean after deliberate introductions to control agricultural pests; highly toxic skin kills native predators; females lay up to 35,000 eggs per clutch; now a cautionary icon of biocontrol failures.

children.name: Rhinella icterica
children.description: Large toad of southeastern Brazil, Argentina, and Paraguay with yellowish flanks; one of the largest Neotropical toads; common across Atlantic Forest edges and the Paraná basin.

children.name: Rhinella schneideri
children.description: One of South America's largest toads, reaching 22 cm; enormous lateral parotoid glands and a heavily ridged skull; inhabits the Cerrado, Chaco, and adjacent Pampas grasslands.

children.name: Rhinella proboscidea
children.description: A widespread Amazonian forest toad named for its slightly upturned snout; occupies leaf-litter of lowland rainforest and breeds in temporary forest pools during the wet season.

children.name: Rhinella margaritifera
children.description: Among the most abundant toads of Amazonian and Guianan forests; medium-sized with rows of white-tipped warts forming a pearl-necklace pattern along the flanks; a significant prey item for larger snakes.

children.name: Rhinella granulosa
children.description: Small and densely granular-skinned toad widely distributed across open habitats in Brazil, Venezuela, and the Guianas; thrives in degraded pastures and second-growth scrub; one of the most drought-tolerant Neotropical toads.

children.name: Rhinella crucifer
children.description: Medium-sized toad of the Atlantic Forest and adjacent Cerrado in southeastern Brazil; dorsum typically brownish with darker blotches; a common peridomestic species.

children.name: Rhinella horribilis
children.description: Recently separated from R. marina; native to Central America and northern South America; among the largest toads in the Americas with highly toxic parotoid secretions that can kill dogs.

children.name: Rhinella poeppigii
children.description: A robust Amazonian toad found in Bolivia, Peru, and Brazil; named after German naturalist Eduard Friedrich Poeppig who collected specimens during his 1820s South American expeditions; inhabits rainforest edges and clearings.

children.name: Rhinella castaneotica
children.description: A small, terrestrial toad of Amazonian Peru and Bolivia; characterised by a rich chestnut-brown dorsum; associated with well-drained terra firme forest floor where it forages among leaf litter.

children.name: Rhinella arenarum
children.description: The most widespread toad in southern South America; occurrs from Bolivia to Patagonia.

children.name: Rhaebo
children.description: A small Neotropical genus of mostly forest-dwelling toads ranging from Central America to Amazonia. They tend to be larger and more smooth-skinned than most bufonids, with reduced parotoid glands.

children.name: Rhaebo guttatus
children.description: A large, handsome toad of Amazonian and Guianan lowland rainforest, adorned with orange-tipped warts on a dark ground colour; primarily active at night on the forest floor.

children.name: Rhaebo haematiticus
children.description: A medium-sized forest toad ranging from Costa Rica to Ecuador; blood-red concealed surfaces of the legs serve as a startling flash display when threatened; inhabits humid lowland and foothill forests.

children.name: Ollotis
children.description: A genus of Central American and Mexican toads recently segregated from Bufo, containing around 18 species of mostly highland and cloud-forest toads.

children.name: Ollotis valliceps
children.description: A widespread and adaptable toad of Mexico and Central America; prominent triangular parotoid glands and a distinctive pale lateral stripe; thrives in disturbed habitats and urban gardens along the Gulf Coast.

children.name: Ollotis nebulifer
children.description: Formerly included within O. valliceps; native to the Texas Gulf Coast and northeastern Mexico; a common suburban toad that has expanded its range significantly due to irrigation and landscaping.

children.name: Incilius
children.description: Central American toads (~35 species), including the legendary golden toad whose extinction became an emblem of the global amphibian crisis. The genus occupies habitats from sea level to the cloud forests of the Cordillera.

children.name: Incilius periglenes
children.description: Brilliant orange male cloud-forest toad of Monteverde, Costa Rica, last seen in 1989 and now extinct; an iconic early casualty of the global amphibian decline linked to chytrid disease and climate-driven drying of cloud-forest pools.

children.name: Incilius coccifer
children.description: A small toad of dry Pacific coastal forests in Central America; female mimics a large ant with its body posture when threatened; one of the smallest Incilius species.

children.name: Incilius canaliferus
children.description: Found in humid to semi-arid lowland forests of Mexico and Guatemala; named for the conspicuous groove between the cranial crests; a secretive species that remains buried during the dry season.

children.name: Incilius alvarius
children.description: The largest toad native to North America, reaching 19 cm; confined to the Sonoran Desert of Arizona, New Mexico, and northwest Mexico; parotoid glands secrete 5-MeO-DMT and bufotenin, making it one of the most pharmacologically notable amphibians.

children.name: Incilius marmoreus
children.description: A handsomely marbled green-and-brown toad of Mexico's Pacific lowlands and foothills; associated with thornscrub and tropical dry forest; breeds explosively in temporary pools following the first heavy summer rains.

children.name: Incilius valliceps
children.description: Common along the Gulf of Mexico from Louisiana to Honduras.

children.name: Cranopsis
children.description: A small genus of Mexican highland toads sometimes treated within Anaxyrus, characterised by enlarged cranial crests and found in oak and pine-oak forests of the Sierra Madre.

children.name: Cranopsis bocourti
children.description: A highland toad of Oaxaca and Chiapas, Mexico, associated with pine-oak forest streams; named in honour of French herpetologist Marie Firmin Bocourt; populations are small and highly fragmented.

children.name: Epidalea
children.description: Small toads with distinctive dorsal markings; includes the natterjack, the loudest amphibian in Europe.

children.name: Epidalea calamita
children.description: A narrow yellow dorsal stripe and the loudest call of any European amphibian, audible for kilometres; runs rather than hops; a Priority Species in Britain and rare across much of its range; breeds in shallow, quickly warming pools.

children.name: Atelopus
children.description: The harlequin toads — small, brightly coloured, diurnal stream toads of the Neotropics, among the amphibians worst hit by the chytrid fungus. Over 80 species are known, many critically endangered or extinct.

children.name: Atelopus zeteki
children.description: Critically endangered (likely extinct in the wild) golden toad endemic to a few Panamanian rivers; a national symbol whose image appears on lottery tickets; secretes potent zetekitoxin; survives only in captive assurance colonies.

children.name: Atelopus varius
children.description: Critically endangered harlequin toad of Costa Rica and Panama; once thought extinct after chytrid decimated populations in the 1990s, then rediscovered in tiny relict populations; coloration varies greatly.

children.name: Atelopus spumarius
children.description: A small Amazonian harlequin toad ranging across lowland Peru, Colombia, and Ecuador; one of the few Atelopus species still regarded as relatively stable; secretes skin toxins but less potent than its highland relatives.

children.name: Atelopus flavescens
children.description: Bright yellow harlequin toad of the Guiana Shield, inhabiting rocky streams in forested highlands of Suriname, French Guiana, and northern Brazil; aposematic coloration warns predators of its toxicity.

children.name: Atelopus limosus
children.description: A recently described Panamanian harlequin toad with striking black-and-yellow banding; among the few Atelopus species holding stable populations after the chytrid wave; associated with clear rocky streams of the Panamanian highlands.

children.name: Atelopus longirostris
children.description: Presumed extinct for decades and now critically endangered; rediscovered in 2016 in a single Ecuadorian cloud-forest locality after an absence of over 30 years; characterised by a distinctively elongated, pointed snout.

children.name: Atelopus zeki
children.description: A critically endangered harlequin frog from the Colombian Andes with vivid black and yellow patterning.

children.name: Atelopus cruciger
children.description: A Venezuelan harlequin frog thought extinct until rediscovered in 2005.

children.name: Peltophryne
children.description: Caribbean toads radiating across the Greater Antilles, with several threatened island endemics whose limited ranges make them vulnerable to habitat loss and introduced species.

children.name: Peltophryne lemur
children.description: Critically endangered toad endemic to Puerto Rico and formerly the Virgin Islands; a flagship for amphibian conservation in the Caribbean, with an active captive-breeding and headstarting programme.

children.name: Peltophryne peltocephala
children.description: The common toad of Cuba and associated small islands; adapted to a range of habitats including urban areas; breeds in a variety of permanent and temporary water bodies; the most abundant bufonid in the Greater Antilles.

children.name: Peltophryne fustiger
children.description: A large Cuban endemic toad found in forests and agricultural areas.

children.name: Peltophryne taladai
children.description: A Cuban species restricted to the Sierra Maestra massif.

children.name: Amietophrynus
children.description: The dominant African toad genus (~44 species), radiating from rainforest to savanna and semi-arid scrub across sub-Saharan Africa. Named in honour of herpetologist Jean-Luc Amiet.

children.name: Amietophrynus regularis
children.description: The most widespread toad in Africa, found from Egypt and Sudan to South Africa; highly adaptable to human-modified landscapes; used in the first clinical pregnancy tests (Hogben test) in the 1930s.

children.name: Amietophrynus rangeri
children.description: A large robust toad of southern Africa, closely related to A. regularis; named after South African naturalist John Ranby; common in gardens, grasslands, and fynbos habitats.

children.name: Amietophrynus gutturalis
children.description: Named for its distinctive guttural croaking call, this toad is common across the savannas of east and southern Africa; extremely tolerant of urbanisation and a dominant garden amphibian in Johannesburg and Nairobi.

children.name: Amietophrynus maculatus
children.description: A small, flattened toad with distinctively smooth dorsal skin compared to other African bufonids; occurs in savanna and forest margins across West and Central Africa; often found near rocky substrates.

children.name: Amietophrynus kisoloensis
children.description: A montane toad of East and Central Africa associated with high-altitude grasslands and moorlands; found in Uganda, Rwanda, and DR Congo; vulnerable to wetland drainage and overgrazing.

children.name: Amietophrynus latifrons
children.description: A large-headed toad of West and Central African lowland rainforest; characterised by a conspicuously broad skull relative to body length; nocturnal and fossorial, emerging at night to forage on invertebrates.

children.name: Vandijkophrynus
children.description: A small genus of South African toads separated from Amietophrynus, named in honour of South African herpetologist Nico van Dijk. Most species are restricted to the southwestern Cape and adjacent Karoo.

children.name: Vandijkophrynus gariepensis
children.description: An arid-adapted toad of the Karoo and Orange River valley, named after the Gariep (Orange) River; tolerates extreme temperature fluctuations and the highly seasonal rainfall of the semi-desert interior of South Africa.

children.name: Vandijkophrynus angusticeps
children.description: A narrow-headed, sandy-coloured toad endemic to the Western Cape and southern Namibia; associated with Fynbos and Succulent Karoo biomes; a rapid coloniser of temporary rain pools after winter rains.

children.name: Schismaderma
children.description: A monotypic African genus distinguished from all other bufonids by having a single species whose parotoid glands are distinctly divided by a groove — a unique anatomical feature giving the genus its name.

children.name: Schismaderma carens
children.description: A distinctive brick-red to pinkish toad of southern and eastern African savannas; lacks the parotoid glands typical of bufonids; terrestrial and highly fossorial, emerging mainly during the wet season to breed in still or slow-moving water.

children.name: Nimbaphrynoides
children.description: A remarkable West African toad genus exhibiting true viviparity — among the only frogs to give live birth to fully formed young. Both species are critically endangered and restricted to the Nimba highlands.

children.name: Nimbaphrynoides occidentalis
children.description: Critically endangered toad restricted to the high-altitude grasslands of Mount Nimba on the Guinea-Ivory Coast-Liberia border; one of very few frogs worldwide that gives birth to live young after a nine-month gestation.

children.name: Nimbaphrynoides liberiensis
children.description: The rarer of the two Nimbaphrynoides species; known from a handful of localities on Mount Nimba in Liberia; critically endangered and threatened by mining activity in the Nimba highland ecosystem.

children.name: Nectophrynoides
children.description: African viviparous toads (~17 species) of montane East Africa, many endemic to single volcanic peaks or mountain ranges in Tanzania. The genus includes direct-developing, ovoviviparous, and truly viviparous species.

children.name: Nectophrynoides tornieri
children.description: A viviparous montane toad of the Eastern Arc Mountains of Tanzania; named after German herpetologist Gustav Tornier; gives birth to fully formed miniature toadlets; threatened by deforestation of its narrow highland range.

children.name: Nectophrynoides viviparus
children.description: Restricted to the Uzungwe and Udzungwa mountains of Tanzania; one of the most thoroughly studied viviparous amphibians; females carry developing young internally through a nine-month gestation period.

children.name: Nectophrynoides asperginis
children.description: Critically endangered (EW in wild) tiny toad entirely dependent on the permanent mist zone of the Kihansi waterfall gorge in Tanzania; a hydroelectric dam that reduced water flow nearly drove it to extinction; a captive colony at the Bronx Zoo became a lifeline for the species.

children.name: Duttaphrynus
children.description: Asian toads separated from Bufo on molecular grounds, including the ubiquitous common toad of South and Southeast Asia. The genus spans the Himalayan foothills, the Indian subcontinent, and coastal Southeast Asia.

children.name: Duttaphrynus melanostictus
children.description: The most widespread toad of South and Southeast Asia; commensal with humans and thriving in cities; recently introduced to Madagascar where its toxins threaten naive native predators unfamiliar with bufonid defences.

children.name: Duttaphrynus himalayanus
children.description: A robust toad of the Himalayan foothills and mid-hills found from Afghanistan to Myanmar; inhabits cool moist montane habitats up to 4000 m elevation; more slender and longer-limbed than D. melanostictus.

children.name: Duttaphrynus stomaticus
children.description: An arid-adapted toad of the Indus River plains and adjacent areas in Pakistan, India, and Afghanistan; tolerates extreme heat and dry conditions; breeds opportunistically following monsoon rains.

children.name: Duttaphrynus scaber
children.description: A small, rough-skinned toad of peninsular India and Sri Lanka; one of the most common amphibians in degraded habitats across the Deccan Plateau; highly resistant to desiccation and capable of surviving in heavily disturbed agricultural landscapes.

children.name: Capensibufo
children.description: A small genus of Cape Peninsula toads endemic to the fynbos biome of South Africa's southwestern Cape; among the most geographically restricted bufonids on Earth.

children.name: Capensibufo rosei
children.description: Critically endangered toad restricted to a single mountain on the Cape Peninsula near Cape Town; named in honour of South African herpetologist Walter Rose; threatened by invasive pine trees altering the hydrology of its breeding streams.

children.name: Poyntonophrynus
children.description: A genus of small, cryptic African toads named in honour of South African herpetologist John Poynton, with about ten species distributed across the savanna and bushveld biomes of southern Africa.

children.name: Poyntonophrynus vertebralis
children.description: A tiny toad with a distinctive pale vertebral stripe occurring in arid and semi-arid grasslands of southern Africa; spends most of its life buried and emerges explosively after summer rains to breed in ephemeral pools.

children.name: Poyntonophrynus fenoulheti
children.description: A diminutive savanna toad found across northeastern South Africa, Zimbabwe, and Mozambique; named after colonial-era naturalist T. Fenoulhet; occupies sandy bushveld habitats and rock outcrops.

children.name: Mertensophryne
children.description: Small East African forest toads named in honour of German herpetologist Robert Mertens. The genus includes several miniaturised species endemic to the Eastern Arc Mountains and coastal forests of Tanzania and Kenya.

children.name: Mertensophryne micranotis
children.description: A tiny, secretive forest-floor toad of coastal forests in Kenya and Tanzania; named for German herpetologist Robert Mertens; direct-developing — eggs hatch directly into toadlets without a free-living tadpole stage.
